title: Demilune Table That Opens Into a Full Circle
summary: A half-round console table that hides a fourth leg and a hinged top — pull both out and it becomes a full circular dining table.
description:
A demilune — a half-round table meant to sit flat against a wall — is a space-saving shape right up until you need to seat six people for dinner. This one solves that with a party trick: the hinged top unfolds into a full circle, and a **fourth leg slides out of the back apron** to catch the load the moment it's no longer against the wall.

The fourth-leg mechanism is the entire reason to build this over a plain demilune. It has to slide smoothly under load and lock into a position that actually supports weight — dry-fit the slide and stop pieces before you commit to final assembly, because this is the one part of the build where "close enough" shows up as a wobbling table leg at dinner.

Built from plywood and MDF rather than solid stock — the arcs and slides are far easier to cut accurately from sheet goods than from a glued-up panel.

tools.note: Cuts the arc-shaped apron supports and the half-round top.
Tapers the legs and rips the aprons; a tapering jig helps.
Holds the arced supports to the legs while the pocket screws draw them in.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Top, aprons, apron supports, leg shelf, and fourth-leg mechanism.

materials.name: 3/4" MDF or plywood, half sheet
materials.note: Extra stock for the arc-shaped apron supports.

materials.name: Edge banding
materials.note: Covers every exposed plywood edge on the top and aprons.

materials.name: Continuous hinge (or 3 hinges)
materials.note: Joins the two half-round top panels.

cutList.note: Cut as a quarter-circle arc from this blank — 21-1/8" radius. Remove a 1-1/2" piece from the center of two of them for the center front leg.
Kerf the length in 1/8" cuts so they curve along the apron supports.
Each panel is cut to a half-circle from this blank; the two panels hinge together.
These are what let the fourth leg travel out from the back apron.
Stop the leg at the correct extended position — without these it can slide out too far or fall free.

steps.title: Cut and taper the legs
steps.body: Cut the four legs to 29-3/4" and taper them on a table saw with a tapering jig or with a jigsaw. Set them aside — they get their apron supports attached in the next step.

steps.title: Cut the arc-shaped apron supports
steps.body:
Mark the center width line on each blank, draw a half circle at 21-1/8" radius, offset that line 3/4", and cut with a jigsaw. Remove a 1-1/2" section from the center of two of the four pieces — that notch is for the center front leg.

Drill pocket holes in each support and attach it to a leg, tapers facing inward, with glue and pocket screws.

steps.title: Cut and fit the front and back aprons
steps.body:
Cut the front apron pieces long, then cut a series of 1/8" kerfs along their length so they can bend to follow the curved apron supports. Cut the back aprons straight — they don't need to curve.

Attach both to the leg/support assemblies with pocket screws.

steps.title: Cut and hinge the top
steps.body: Cut the two half-round top panels from the plywood blanks and join them with a continuous hinge (or three hinges spaced evenly) along the straight edge — this is the fold line that lets the top swing open into a full circle.

steps.title: Build the fourth-leg slide mechanism
steps.body:
Cut the fourth-leg aprons, slides, and stops per the cut list and assemble them so the extra leg can travel out from inside the back apron and lock at a position directly under the opened top.

Dry-fit this before final glue-up. If the leg binds or doesn't land squarely under the load when extended, the table will wobble the first time someone leans on the open half.

steps.title: Assemble, edge-band, and finish
steps.body: Attach the leg shelf and its supports between the legs for extra rigidity, then bring the top assembly onto the base. Edge-band every exposed plywood edge, sand the whole piece, and finish.
